title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
Note



Audience: Students, bioinformatics researchers, clinical programmers, data scientists, pharmaceutical professionals, and software engineers.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
1. Introduction
Nextflow is a workflow management system for running computational pipelines. It connects analysis steps, runs them in parallel, and scales from a laptop to the cloud.

Why Nextflow exists
Bioinformatics and data pipelines often involve many steps, large datasets, and many samples. Shell scripts become hard to maintain, restart, and scale. Nextflow was built to solve reproducible, portable, parallel workflow execution.


The problem Nextflow solves








Problem
Without Nextflow
With Nextflow




Parallelism
Manual job arrays
Automatic parallel tasks


Failures
Restart from scratch
Resume from last successful step


Portability
Scripts tied to one cluster
Profiles for local, AWS, GCP


Reproducibility
Ad-hoc environments
Docker/Singularity/Conda per step


Complexity
Spaghetti bash scripts
Structured processes and channels





Where Nextflow is used in industry

Academic genomics cores — RNA-seq, WGS, single-cell pipelines
Pharma bioinformatics — target discovery, biomarker pipelines
CROs & sequencing vendors — standardized nf-core pipelines
Public health — pathogen surveillance (e.g., EPI2ME, viral pipelines)
Cloud-native labs — AWS Batch, Google Life Sciences, Kubernetes

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
2. Learning Objectives

By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to

Explain what Nextflow is and why bioinformatics teams adopt it
Install Nextflow and run a hello-world workflow locally
Define processes, channels, and workflows
Configure Docker containers for reproducible tool execution
Run pipelines on AWS and GCP using profiles
Integrate R and Python scripts into Nextflow processes
Map Nextflow to FASTQ-to-report bioinformatics workflows
Apply best practices for clinical and regulated environments
Troubleshoot common Nextflow errors
Design a simple QC pipeline as a mini project

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
3. Why is this important?

Why should someone learn Nextflow?
Nextflow is the de facto standard for portable bioinformatics pipelines. nf-core alone has 100+ community pipelines used worldwide.


Why do companies use it?

Scale — process hundreds of samples in parallel
Resume — -resume saves time and cloud cost after failures
Portability — same pipeline on laptop, HPC, or cloud via profiles
Community — nf-core, Seqera Tower ecosystem



Why is it important in bioinformatics?
Genomics workflows are multi-step and data-heavy. Nextflow manages dependencies, parallelization, and containerized tools automatically.


Why is it important in clinical programming?
While SAS/R dominate TLFs, Nextflow supports omics sub-studies, biomarker pipelines, and reproducible secondary analyses linked to trials.


Why is it useful in data science?
Nextflow orchestrates feature engineering, model training, and batch scoring at scale — especially when combined with Docker and cloud executors.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
4. Architecture Diagram

Nextflow core architecture













Pipeline data flow

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
5. Installation

Windows (WSL2 recommended)
Nextflow runs best on Linux/macOS. On Windows, use WSL2:
# Inside WSL Ubuntu
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
sudo mv nextflow /usr/local/bin/
nextflow -v
Install Java 11+:
sudo apt-get install -y openjdk-17-jre
java -version


macOS
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
sudo mv nextflow /usr/local/bin/
brew install openjdk@17
nextflow -v


Linux
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
sudo mv nextflow /usr/local/bin/
sudo apt-get install -y openjdk-17-jre
nextflow -v
Verify:
nextflow run hello






Tip



Optional: Install Docker for containerized processes.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
6. Basic Concepts

Process







Definition
A single computational step with inputs, outputs, and a script


Analogy
One station on a factory assembly line


Industry example
FastQC on each FASTQ file in parallel


Best practice
One logical task per process; pin container versions





Channel







Definition
An asynchronous queue connecting processes with data


Analogy
A conveyor belt carrying work items


Industry example
One channel item per patient sample


Best practice
Use tuple channels for paired-end reads





Workflow







Definition
The DAG (graph) linking processes


Analogy
The master floor plan of the factory


Industry example
QC → align → call variants → annotate


Best practice
Keep main.nf readable; modularize with include





Executor







Definition
Backend that runs tasks (local, slurm, awsbatch, etc.)


Analogy
The HR department assigning workers to stations


Industry example
awsbatch for cloud scale


Best practice
Use profiles in nextflow.config





Profile







Definition
Named configuration bundle (executor, containers, paths)


Analogy
A preset mode: “laptop” vs “cloud”


Industry example
-profile docker,aws


Best practice
Never hardcode credentials in profiles





Container directive



Definition
Docker/Singularity image used by a process


Analogy
The standardized toolbox for that station


Industry example
biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8


Best practice
Always specify full image tags





work/ directory



Definition
Cached task directories for resume and debugging


Analogy
The workshop floor where in-progress jobs live


Industry example
Inspected when a variant-calling step fails


Best practice
Use shared workDir on cloud; enable -resume

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
7. Hands-on Examples

Example 1 — Hello World
Problem: Verify Nextflow runs locally.
Solution: Create main.nf:
#!/usr/bin/env nextflow

process sayHello {
  output:
    path 'hello.txt'

  script:
  """
  echo "Hello from Nextflow" > hello.txt
  """
}

workflow {
  sayHello()
}
Run:
nextflow run main.nf
cat work/*/hello.txt
Expected output: Hello from Nextflow



Example 2 — Parallel sample processing
Problem: Process multiple samples in parallel.
Solution:
params.samples = ['S1', 'S2', 'S3']

process greetSample {
  input:
    val sample

  output:
    stdout

  script:
  """
  echo "Processing sample ${sample}"
  """
}

workflow {
  Channel.fromList(params.samples) | greetSample | view
}
Expected result: Three lines, order may vary (parallel execution).



Example 3 — Dockerized FastQC
Problem: Run FastQC in a reproducible container.
Solution:
process runFastqc {
  container 'biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8'

  input:
    path fastq

  output:
    path "*.html"

  script:
  """
  fastqc ${fastq}
  """
}

workflow {
  Channel.fromPath('*.fastq.gz') | runFastqc
}
Expected result: HTML QC reports per FASTQ file.



Example 4 — Python in Nextflow
Problem: Run a Python script inside a pipeline.
Solution:
process runPython {
  container 'python:3.11-slim'

  input:
    path csv

  output:
    path 'out.txt'

  script:
  """
  python -c "import pandas as pd; df=pd.read_csv('${csv}'); print(df.shape)" > out.txt
  """
}



Example 5 — R analysis step
Problem: Execute analysis.R with Dockerized R.
Solution:
process runR {
  container 'rocker/r-ver:4.3'

  input:
    path input_csv

  output:
    path 'output.csv'

  script:
  """
  Rscript analysis.R ${input_csv} output.csv
  """
}
analysis.R:
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
dat <- read.csv(args[1])
write.csv(summary(dat), args[2])

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
9. Bioinformatics Workflow











Role of Nextflow: Orchestrates each step, runs samples in parallel, manages retries/resume, and invokes Docker containers per process.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
10. Clinical Programming Workflow











How Nextflow helps: Primary TLFs often use SAS/R, but omics, digital endpoints, and RWE pipelines increasingly use Nextflow for reproducible secondary analyses linked to trials.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
11. Best Practices

DO

Use -resume during development
Pin container tags on every process
Store configs in nextflow.config with profiles
Test on small datasets first
Publish reports: -with-report, -with-trace, -with-timeline
Use nf-core pipelines when applicable
Keep secrets in environment variables or secret managers



DON’T

Hardcode cloud credentials in Git
Run production on :latest containers
Ignore failed tasks in work/ without logs
Mix dev and prod buckets/workDirs
Process PHI without access controls on cloud storage



Performance tips

Maximize parallelism with channels
Use appropriate cloud instance types per process
Store references genomes on shared fast storage



Security tips

Private buckets, VPC endpoints, IAM least privilege
Encrypt data at rest and in transit
Audit Tower/Nextflow run metadata



Optimization tips

Cache aggressively with -resume
Use errorStrategy and maxRetries wisely
Profile with -with-trace before scaling

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
12. Common Mistakes








Mistake
Reason
Solution




Java not installed
Nextflow requires JVM
Install Java 11+


Docker not available
Container engine missing
Install Docker; enable in config


Empty channel
Wrong glob or path
Check Channel.fromPath patterns


Permission denied on cloud
IAM misconfiguration
Fix roles for S3/Batch/GCP


Out of memory
Instance too small
Increase memory in process or instance


Stale resume cache
Changed script but same hash confusion
Use -resume carefully; delete work dir if needed


Missing input files
Relative path issues
Use absolute paths or params.input


Slow local runs
Too much parallelism on laptop
Limit maxForks in config

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
13. Troubleshooting Guide








Error
Cause
Solution




Unable to acquire lock
Another Nextflow run active
Wait or remove .nextflow.lock safely


Process exceeded memory
OOM kill
Increase memory directive


Cannot find Java
JAVA_HOME unset
Install JRE; export JAVA_HOME


Docker not found
Docker daemon down
Start Docker Desktop


Access Denied (S3/GCS)
Credentials/permissions
Fix IAM; verify bucket


Task hangs
Network/storage issue
Check .command.log in work dir


Container pull fails
Wrong tag or no network
Verify image name; login to registry



Diagnostic commands:
nextflow log
nextflow run main.nf -resume -with-trace trace.txt
# Inspect task folder:
cat work/ab/cd1234*/.command.err
cat work/ab/cd1234*/.command.log

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
14. Interview Questions

Beginner
Q1: What is Nextflow?
A: A workflow manager for parallel, reproducible data and bioinformatics pipelines.
Q2: What is a process?
A: A pipeline step with defined inputs, outputs, and execution script.
Q3: Why use containers with Nextflow?
A: To pin software versions and ensure reproducibility across environments.


Intermediate
Q4: What does -resume do?
A: Reuses cached successful tasks from previous runs, rerunning only failed or changed steps.
Q5: Explain channels.
A: Queues that pass data between processes asynchronously.
Q6: How do profiles work?
A: Named configuration sets selected via -profile for different executors and settings.


Advanced
Q7: Compare Nextflow with Snakemake.
A: Both orchestrate workflows; Nextflow emphasizes dataflow channels and cloud-native executors; Snakemake uses rule graphs — choice depends on team and ecosystem.
Q8: How would you run Nextflow on AWS Batch?
A: Configure process.executor = 'awsbatch', S3 workDir, IAM roles, and container registry access in nextflow.config.
Q9: How do you handle secrets?
A: Environment variables, AWS Secrets Manager, Vault — never commit keys to Git.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
15. Cheat Sheet
# Install / update
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
nextflow -v

# Run
nextflow run main.nf
nextflow run main.nf -profile docker
nextflow run main.nf -resume
nextflow run main.nf -with-report report.html

# Params
nextflow run main.nf --input data/ --outdir results/

# Info
nextflow log
nextflow info
nextflow pull nf-core/rnaseq



Concept
Syntax




Process input
input: path file


Process output
output: path "*.bam"


Container
container 'image:tag'


Channel from list
Channel.fromList([...])


View channel
\| view

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
16. Summary

Nextflow orchestrates multi-step computational pipelines
Processes run tasks; channels pass data; workflows connect steps
It enables parallelism, resume, and cloud scaling
Docker integration ensures reproducible tool environments
Profiles switch between local, AWS, GCP, and HPC execution
nf-core provides production-grade community pipelines
Bioinformatics workflows from FASTQ to report map naturally to Nextflow
Clinical omics sub-studies benefit from reproducible Nextflow pipelines
Use reports, pinned containers, and secure cloud configuration
Troubleshoot via work/ logs and -with-trace
Learn Docker first, then Nextflow, then Kubernetes/AWS for deployment at scale

Recommended next: Kubernetes for cluster orchestration, AWS for cloud execution, or Quarto for publishing pipeline reports.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
20. Mini Project — Two-Step QC Pipeline

Problem statement
Build a Nextflow pipeline that greets each sample ID and simulates a QC pass — foundation for real FastQC integration.


Architecture













Folder structure
nf-qc-demo/
├── main.nf
├── nextflow.config
└── data/
    └── samples.txt


Implementation (main.nf)
params.samples = file('data/samples.txt').readLines()

process greet {
  input:
    val sample
  output:
    tuple val(sample), path("${sample}.txt")

  script:
  """
  echo "QC passed for ${sample}" > ${sample}.txt
  """
}

workflow {
  Channel.fromList(params.samples) | greet | view
}
data/samples.txt:
SAMPLE001
SAMPLE002
SAMPLE003


Commands
nextflow run main.nf -with-report report.html


Expected output
Three .txt files in work/ and a timeline/report HTML.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
21. Exercises

10 practice questions

What three core concepts define a Nextflow pipeline?
What flag resumes a failed pipeline efficiently?
How do you specify a Docker image for a process?
What is the purpose of the work/ directory?
Name one nf-core pipeline and its use case.
How does Nextflow achieve parallelism?
What is a profile?
Where are task stderr logs stored?
Why pin container versions?
How does Nextflow relate to Docker?



5 assignments

Add a third process to the mini project that aggregates QC results.
Run hello-world with -with-timeline timeline.html.
Configure a docker profile in nextflow.config.
Pull and inspect nf-core/rnaseq parameters (do not run full pipeline).
Document an AWS profile skeleton (no credentials).



3 mini projects

Wrap FastQC in Nextflow for all *.fastq.gz in a folder.
Add a Python pandas summary process after QC.
Generate a Quarto report from pipeline outputs.



1 capstone project
Design a 4-step genomics pipeline (QC → align → stats → report) with Docker containers, -resume, and HTML reports — use mock scripts if data is unavailable.

title: Nextflow — Professional Tutorial for Bioinformatics & Cloud
Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Welcome! This tutorial is written for people who are completely new to Quarto and may also have no background in R.
Quarto is a tool for creating: - reports - blogs - websites - books - presentations - PDFs and Word documents
You write content in simple plain text using Markdown, and Quarto turns it into polished output.



Quarto is an open-source publishing system.
In simple words: - You write text in a file - You can add headings, lists, images, tables, and code - Quarto converts that file into HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more
You do not need to know R to start using Quarto.
Quarto can work with: - plain Markdown only - Python - R - Julia - Observable JavaScript
If you are a beginner, you can first learn Quarto as a document writing tool without writing any code.




Quarto is useful because it helps you: - write once and create multiple output formats - keep text, code, and results in one place - create professional-looking documents - make reproducible reports - build personal websites, course notes, and books


You can use Quarto to create: - class notes - project reports - resumes - research papers - tutorials - data analysis reports - technical blogs - slide presentations





No.
This is very important.
You can use Quarto without knowing R.
There are two common ways to use Quarto:


You write Markdown and create a document. No programming needed.



Later, if you want, you can add Python or R code blocks.
So if you are a complete beginner, start with text-only documents.





To use Quarto comfortably, install the following:



Quarto
A text editor or IDE





VS Code (great for beginners)
RStudio / Positron (optional, even if you do not use R)





Python if you want to run Python code later
R if you want to run R code later
TinyTeX or TeX Live if you want to create PDFs




Go to the official site: - https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Download and install Quarto for your operating system.



Open your terminal and run:
quarto --version
If Quarto is installed correctly, you will see a version number.





Markdown is a very simple way to format text.
Instead of clicking buttons for bold headings or lists, you type small symbols.




# Main Title
## Section Title
### Smaller Section



**bold text**
*italic text*



- item one
- item two
- item three



1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step



[Quarto Website](https://quarto.org)
This is one reason Quarto is beginner-friendly.






Quarto documents usually use the .qmd extension.
Create a file called:
hello.qmd
Add this content:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

## About Me

I am learning Quarto.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start


That section is called YAML.
It stores document settings like: - title - author - date - output format
In this example: - title sets the title of the document - format: html tells Quarto to create an HTML page





To convert a .qmd file into output, Quarto uses the word render.


Run:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto will create an HTML file, usually:
hello.html
Open that file in your browser.



If you use VS Code with Quarto installed, you can often preview or render directly from the editor.





A simple Quarto document has two main parts:


This controls document settings.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-06-12"
format: html
---



This is where you write: - headings - paragraphs - lists - images - tables - code blocks







# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3



Just write normal text.



Leave a blank line between paragraphs.



**bold**
*italic*
`code`



> This is an important note.



---



- apple
- banana
- orange



1. Install Quarto
2. Create a file
3. Render the file





You can add an image like this:
![A sample image](images/example.png)
Explanation: - text inside [] is alternate text - text inside () is the image path
If your image is in a folder called images, the file path might be: - images/photo.jpg - images/chart.png




[Visit Quarto](https://quarto.org)
This creates a clickable link.




Simple Markdown table:
| Tool   | Purpose            |
|--------|--------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents   |
| VS Code| Edit files         |
| Python | Run code optional  |
This becomes a formatted table in the output.




Even if you do not know programming yet, it is helpful to understand the idea.
A code block is a section where you write code.


```bash
quarto render hello.qmd

This only displays code.

### Executable code block
If you have Python or R installed, Quarto can run code blocks and show results.

Example with Python:

````markdown
```python
print("Hello from Python")

If Python is available, Quarto can run it and include the output.

Again: **this is optional** for beginners.

---

## 14. A text-only beginner example

Create a file named `intro.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Introduction to Quarto"
format: html
---

# Welcome

Quarto helps you create beautiful documents.

## What I learned

- Quarto uses `.qmd` files
- Markdown is easy to write
- I can create HTML pages
- I do not need R to begin

## My Next Step

I will learn how to add images and tables.
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render intro.qmd
```

---

## 15. Creating different output formats

One powerful feature of Quarto is that you can generate different types of output.

### HTML
```yaml
format: html
```

### PDF
```yaml
format: pdf
```

### Word
```yaml
format: docx
```

### Multiple formats
```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

Then run:

```bash
quarto render yourfile.qmd
```

### Important note about PDF
Creating PDFs usually requires a LaTeX installation such as TinyTeX.

If PDF rendering fails, start with HTML first.

---

## 16. Working with sections

A bigger document can be organized into sections.

Example:

```markdown
# Chapter 1
## Topic 1.1
## Topic 1.2

# Chapter 2
## Topic 2.1
```

This structure helps readers navigate your content.

In HTML output, Quarto may also generate a table of contents.

---

## 17. Adding a table of contents

You can enable it in YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Tutorial"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---
```

This adds a table of contents automatically.

---

## 18. Changing theme and appearance

For HTML documents, you can choose themes.

Example:

```yaml
---
title: "Styled Document"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Some common themes:
- cosmo
- litera
- flatly
- darkly
- minty

You can experiment and see which one you like.

---

## 19. Creating a presentation

Quarto can create slides too.

Example:

```markdown
---
title: "My First Presentation"
format: revealjs
---

# Slide 1
Welcome to my presentation.

# Slide 2
- Point one
- Point two
```

Render it and Quarto will create a slide deck.

---

## 20. Creating a website

Quarto can also build websites.

A basic website project often includes:
- `_quarto.yml`
- `index.qmd`
- other `.qmd` pages

Example `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Website"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

Example `index.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.
```

This is more advanced, but it shows how powerful Quarto is.

---

## 21. What is `_quarto.yml`?

When you work on a Quarto project with multiple files, `_quarto.yml` is the main configuration file.

It can define:
- project type
- website settings
- book settings
- output format defaults
- themes
- navigation

Think of it as the project control file.

---

## 22. How Quarto is different from Word

In Word, you usually format things visually.

In Quarto, you write simple plain text with lightweight formatting.

### Benefits of Quarto
- easier version control with Git and GitHub
- reusable documents
- cleaner structure
- can combine writing and code
- easier to publish online

At first it may feel unfamiliar, but many people find it easier over time.

---

## 23. Beginner workflow

Here is a simple beginner workflow:

1. Install Quarto
2. Create a `.qmd` file
3. Write Markdown content
4. Add YAML at the top
5. Render the file to HTML
6. Open the output in a browser
7. Edit and render again

This is enough to get started.

---

## 24. Common beginner mistakes

### Mistake 1: Forgetting the YAML lines
YAML must start and end with `---`.

### Mistake 2: Wrong file extension
Use `.qmd`, not `.txt`.

### Mistake 3: PDF not rendering
This often happens because LaTeX is not installed.

### Mistake 4: Broken image path
Make sure the image file path is correct.

### Mistake 5: Expecting code to run without installing Python or R
If you want executable code blocks, you need the related language installed.

---

## 25. Example: A small personal profile page

Create `profile.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "My Profile"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

# About Me

My name is Praveen.

I am learning Quarto to create documents and websites.

## Skills I Want to Learn

- Quarto
- Markdown
- GitHub
- Python

## Favorite Resources

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org)
- [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render profile.qmd
```

---

## 26. Example: A study notes document

```markdown
---
title: "Study Notes"
format: html
---

# Chapter 1

This chapter explains the basics.

## Key Points

- Point A
- Point B
- Point C

## Summary

This chapter introduced the main ideas.
```

This is useful for students and self-learners.

---

## 27. If you later want to learn code in Quarto

Once you are comfortable with text and Markdown, you can gradually add code.

### Python example

````markdown
```python
name = "Praveen"
print("Hello", name)
```



```r
x <- 10
x + 5
```
But remember: learning Quarto does not require learning R first.






Start with HTML output
Write short documents first
Focus on Markdown before advanced features
Keep files organized in folders
Use meaningful file names
Render often to check your output
Learn one feature at a time





Here is a beginner-friendly order:


Learn: - headings - paragraphs - bold and italic - lists - links



Learn: - YAML title and format - rendering to HTML - adding images - adding tables



Learn: - themes - table of contents - multiple pages - websites



Optional: - Python code blocks - R code blocks - PDFs - presentations - books







No. Anyone can use it for writing and publishing.



No. You can start with plain text and Markdown.



Yes, Quarto is open source and free to use.



Yes.



Yes.



No. VS Code works very well too.





Before starting, make sure you can do these:

install Quarto
create a .qmd file
write a title in YAML
add headings and lists
render to HTML
open the output file

If you can do these, you are already using Quarto successfully.




Quarto is a beginner-friendly publishing tool.
Even if you: - do not know R - do not know programming - have never written Markdown before
You can still start learning Quarto today.
The best way to learn is: - create a small .qmd file - write simple content - render it - improve it step by step
Start simple, and build confidence gradually.




Try this today:

Install Quarto
Create hello.qmd
Paste this content:

---
title: "Hello Quarto"
format: html
---

# My First Page

I am learning Quarto.

- I can write text
- I can create headings
- I can render HTML

Run:

quarto render hello.qmd
That is your first Quarto success.

If you want, the next tutorial can be about: - Quarto for complete beginners using VS Code - How to create a Quarto website step by step - Markdown for absolute beginners - Quarto with Python for beginners

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system.
In simple words: - You write text in a file - You can add headings, lists, images, tables, and code - Quarto converts that file into HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more
You do not need to know R to start using Quarto.
Quarto can work with: - plain Markdown only - Python - R - Julia - Observable JavaScript
If you are a beginner, you can first learn Quarto as a document writing tool without writing any code.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto is useful because it helps you: - write once and create multiple output formats - keep text, code, and results in one place - create professional-looking documents - make reproducible reports - build personal websites, course notes, and books


You can use Quarto to create: - class notes - project reports - resumes - research papers - tutorials - data analysis reports - technical blogs - slide presentations

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
No.
This is very important.
You can use Quarto without knowing R.
There are two common ways to use Quarto:


You write Markdown and create a document. No programming needed.



Later, if you want, you can add Python or R code blocks.
So if you are a complete beginner, start with text-only documents.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
To use Quarto comfortably, install the following:



Quarto
A text editor or IDE





VS Code (great for beginners)
RStudio / Positron (optional, even if you do not use R)





Python if you want to run Python code later
R if you want to run R code later
TinyTeX or TeX Live if you want to create PDFs




Go to the official site: - https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Download and install Quarto for your operating system.



Open your terminal and run:
quarto --version
If Quarto is installed correctly, you will see a version number.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Markdown is a very simple way to format text.
Instead of clicking buttons for bold headings or lists, you type small symbols.




# Main Title
## Section Title
### Smaller Section



**bold text**
*italic text*



- item one
- item two
- item three



1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step



[Quarto Website](https://quarto.org)
This is one reason Quarto is beginner-friendly.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto documents usually use the .qmd extension.
Create a file called:
hello.qmd
Add this content:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

## About Me

I am learning Quarto.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start


That section is called YAML.
It stores document settings like: - title - author - date - output format
In this example: - title sets the title of the document - format: html tells Quarto to create an HTML page

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
To convert a .qmd file into output, Quarto uses the word render.


Run:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto will create an HTML file, usually:
hello.html
Open that file in your browser.



If you use VS Code with Quarto installed, you can often preview or render directly from the editor.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
A simple Quarto document has two main parts:


This controls document settings.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-06-12"
format: html
---



This is where you write: - headings - paragraphs - lists - images - tables - code blocks

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3



Just write normal text.



Leave a blank line between paragraphs.



**bold**
*italic*
`code`



> This is an important note.



---



- apple
- banana
- orange



1. Install Quarto
2. Create a file
3. Render the file

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
You can add an image like this:
![A sample image](images/example.png)
Explanation: - text inside [] is alternate text - text inside () is the image path
If your image is in a folder called images, the file path might be: - images/photo.jpg - images/chart.png

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
[Visit Quarto](https://quarto.org)
This creates a clickable link.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Simple Markdown table:
| Tool   | Purpose            |
|--------|--------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents   |
| VS Code| Edit files         |
| Python | Run code optional  |
This becomes a formatted table in the output.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Even if you do not know programming yet, it is helpful to understand the idea.
A code block is a section where you write code.


```bash
quarto render hello.qmd

This only displays code.

### Executable code block
If you have Python or R installed, Quarto can run code blocks and show results.

Example with Python:

````markdown
```python
print("Hello from Python")

If Python is available, Quarto can run it and include the output.

Again: **this is optional** for beginners.

---

## 14. A text-only beginner example

Create a file named `intro.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Introduction to Quarto"
format: html
---

# Welcome

Quarto helps you create beautiful documents.

## What I learned

- Quarto uses `.qmd` files
- Markdown is easy to write
- I can create HTML pages
- I do not need R to begin

## My Next Step

I will learn how to add images and tables.
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render intro.qmd
```

---

## 15. Creating different output formats

One powerful feature of Quarto is that you can generate different types of output.

### HTML
```yaml
format: html
```

### PDF
```yaml
format: pdf
```

### Word
```yaml
format: docx
```

### Multiple formats
```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

Then run:

```bash
quarto render yourfile.qmd
```

### Important note about PDF
Creating PDFs usually requires a LaTeX installation such as TinyTeX.

If PDF rendering fails, start with HTML first.

---

## 16. Working with sections

A bigger document can be organized into sections.

Example:

```markdown
# Chapter 1
## Topic 1.1
## Topic 1.2

# Chapter 2
## Topic 2.1
```

This structure helps readers navigate your content.

In HTML output, Quarto may also generate a table of contents.

---

## 17. Adding a table of contents

You can enable it in YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Tutorial"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---
```

This adds a table of contents automatically.

---

## 18. Changing theme and appearance

For HTML documents, you can choose themes.

Example:

```yaml
---
title: "Styled Document"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Some common themes:
- cosmo
- litera
- flatly
- darkly
- minty

You can experiment and see which one you like.

---

## 19. Creating a presentation

Quarto can create slides too.

Example:

```markdown
---
title: "My First Presentation"
format: revealjs
---

# Slide 1
Welcome to my presentation.

# Slide 2
- Point one
- Point two
```

Render it and Quarto will create a slide deck.

---

## 20. Creating a website

Quarto can also build websites.

A basic website project often includes:
- `_quarto.yml`
- `index.qmd`
- other `.qmd` pages

Example `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Website"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

Example `index.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.
```

This is more advanced, but it shows how powerful Quarto is.

---

## 21. What is `_quarto.yml`?

When you work on a Quarto project with multiple files, `_quarto.yml` is the main configuration file.

It can define:
- project type
- website settings
- book settings
- output format defaults
- themes
- navigation

Think of it as the project control file.

---

## 22. How Quarto is different from Word

In Word, you usually format things visually.

In Quarto, you write simple plain text with lightweight formatting.

### Benefits of Quarto
- easier version control with Git and GitHub
- reusable documents
- cleaner structure
- can combine writing and code
- easier to publish online

At first it may feel unfamiliar, but many people find it easier over time.

---

## 23. Beginner workflow

Here is a simple beginner workflow:

1. Install Quarto
2. Create a `.qmd` file
3. Write Markdown content
4. Add YAML at the top
5. Render the file to HTML
6. Open the output in a browser
7. Edit and render again

This is enough to get started.

---

## 24. Common beginner mistakes

### Mistake 1: Forgetting the YAML lines
YAML must start and end with `---`.

### Mistake 2: Wrong file extension
Use `.qmd`, not `.txt`.

### Mistake 3: PDF not rendering
This often happens because LaTeX is not installed.

### Mistake 4: Broken image path
Make sure the image file path is correct.

### Mistake 5: Expecting code to run without installing Python or R
If you want executable code blocks, you need the related language installed.

---

## 25. Example: A small personal profile page

Create `profile.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "My Profile"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

# About Me

My name is Praveen.

I am learning Quarto to create documents and websites.

## Skills I Want to Learn

- Quarto
- Markdown
- GitHub
- Python

## Favorite Resources

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org)
- [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render profile.qmd
```

---

## 26. Example: A study notes document

```markdown
---
title: "Study Notes"
format: html
---

# Chapter 1

This chapter explains the basics.

## Key Points

- Point A
- Point B
- Point C

## Summary

This chapter introduced the main ideas.
```

This is useful for students and self-learners.

---

## 27. If you later want to learn code in Quarto

Once you are comfortable with text and Markdown, you can gradually add code.

### Python example

````markdown
```python
name = "Praveen"
print("Hello", name)
```



```r
x <- 10
x + 5
```
But remember: learning Quarto does not require learning R first.

title: Pharmaverse — Clinical R & ADaM Ecosystem

1. Introduction

Back to home

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
Note



Audience: Students, bioinformatics researchers, clinical programmers, data scientists, pharmaceutical professionals, and software engineers — including readers with little or no prior experience.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
1. Introduction
Docker is a platform that packages an application and everything it needs to run — code, libraries, settings — into a portable unit called a container.

Why Docker exists
Before containers, teams struggled with a familiar problem: “It works on my machine.” Different laptops, servers, and cloud environments had different software versions. Results changed. Audits failed. Pipelines broke in production.
Docker was created to make software portable, repeatable, and isolated.


The problem Docker solves








Problem
Without Docker
With Docker




Environment differences
Results vary by machine
Same image → same behavior


Complex installs
Days installing bioinformatics tools
Pull a pre-built image in minutes


Reproducibility
Hard to prove what ran
Versioned images document the environment


Isolation
Tools conflict on one server
Each container runs independently





Where Docker is used in industry

Pharmaceutical R&D — reproducible ML and cheminformatics environments
Clinical programming — fixed R/SAS/Python stacks for TLF generation
Bioinformatics — pipelines for genomics, proteomics, and multi-omics
Healthcare AI — deploying models in controlled containers
Cloud & DevOps — AWS ECS, Google Cloud Run, Kubernetes workloads







Tip



Beginner takeaway: Docker is a standardized box for software. Same box, same result — on your laptop, a lab server, or the cloud.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
2. Learning Objectives

By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to

Explain what Docker is and why regulated industries use it
Install Docker Desktop on Windows, macOS, or Linux
Distinguish images, containers, registries, volumes, and networks
Build a custom image using a Dockerfile
Run, stop, and remove containers from the command line
Mount data safely without baking sensitive files into images
Describe where Docker fits in bioinformatics and clinical programming workflows
Apply industry best practices for security, GxP, and reproducibility
Troubleshoot common Docker errors
Complete a mini project: a reproducible clinical summary container

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
3. Why is this important?

Why should someone learn Docker?
Docker is one of the most requested skills in bioinformatics, data engineering, and DevOps job postings. It turns fragile setups into shareable, testable environments.


Why do companies use it?

Speed — onboard new analysts in hours, not weeks
Compliance — versioned images support audit trails
Scale — same container runs on one laptop or 1,000 cloud nodes
Cost — efficient resource use compared to full virtual machines



Why is it important in bioinformatics?
Genomics pipelines combine dozens of tools (FastQC, BWA, GATK, bcftools). Docker (and Biocontainers) pins exact versions so published results can be reproduced.


Why is it important in clinical programming?
Clinical trial analysis must be reproducible. Docker ensures the same R packages, SAS components, or Python libraries are used for every database lock and QC re-run.


Why is it useful in data science?
Data scientists package models with dependencies so staging and production environments match — reducing “model works locally but fails in deployment” issues.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
4. Architecture Diagram

Docker core architecture













Life-sciences workflow placement

















Important



Screenshot placeholder: Add a screenshot of Docker Desktop showing a running container after completing Section 5.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
5. Installation

Windows

Download Docker Desktop for Windows
Enable WSL 2 if prompted (recommended)
Install and restart
Open Docker Desktop and wait until status shows Running

Verify:
docker --version
docker run hello-world


macOS

Download Docker Desktop for Mac (Apple Silicon or Intel)
Drag Docker to Applications and launch
Accept permissions

Verify:
docker --version
docker run hello-world


Linux (Ubuntu/Debian example)
# Install Docker Engine
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y ca-certificates curl
sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings
sudo curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc
sudo chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc

echo \
  "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
  $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | \
  sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin

# Optional: run without sudo
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker
Verify:
docker --version
docker run hello-world






Warning



Corporate laptops may block Docker. Contact IT before installing in pharmaceutical or hospital environments.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
6. Basic Concepts

Image







Definition
Read-only template containing an app and its dependencies


Analogy
A sealed recipe kit with pre-measured ingredients


Industry example
rocker/r-ver:4.3.2 for a locked clinical R environment


Best practice
Always use specific version tags — never :latest in production





Container







Definition
A running instance of an image


Analogy
The actual meal cooked from the recipe kit


Industry example
One container per study analysis run


Best practice
Use --rm for temporary jobs; name containers in long-running services





Registry







Definition
Storage and distribution system for images


Analogy
A warehouse catalog of standardized boxes


Industry example
Private AWS ECR registry for validated pharma images


Best practice
Use private registries for internal and GxP workloads





Docker Hub







Definition
Public registry maintained by Docker


Analogy
An app store for container images


Industry example
Pulling official python:3.11-slim base images


Best practice
Prefer verified publishers; scan images for vulnerabilities





Dockerfile







Definition
Text file with step-by-step instructions to build an image


Analogy
The master recipe card


Industry example
Documented build file archived with study deliverables


Best practice
Keep Dockerfiles in version control; review changes like code





Volume







Definition
Persistent storage mounted into a container


Analogy
A USB drive plugged into the box


Industry example
Mount clinical CSV input and TLF output folders


Best practice
Never store patient data inside the image — mount at runtime





Network







Definition
Virtual network allowing containers to communicate


Analogy
Phone lines between offices in the same building


Industry example
Connect a Shiny app container to a PostgreSQL container


Best practice
Use custom networks; avoid exposing unnecessary ports

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
7. Hands-on Examples

Example 1 — Hello World
Problem: Confirm Docker is installed and working.
Solution: Run the official test image.
docker run hello-world
Expected output: Message containing Hello from Docker!
Explanation: Docker downloaded a tiny image, started a container, printed output, and exited.



Example 2 — Run R without installing R
Problem: You need R 4.3 but do not want to install it locally.
Solution:
docker run --rm rocker/r-ver:4.3 R --version
Expected output: R version 4.3.x ...
Explanation: The container includes R; your host machine stays unchanged.



Example 3 — Interactive Python shell
Problem: Test Python in an isolated environment.
Solution:
docker run -it --rm python:3.11-slim python
print("Docker Python ready")
exit()
Expected result: Python REPL opens, prints message, exits cleanly.



Example 4 — Build a clinical summary image
Problem: Create a reproducible environment for summarizing patient ages.
Solution:
Create summarize.R:
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
dat <- read.csv(args[1])
out <- data.frame(
  n = nrow(dat),
  mean_age = round(mean(dat$age, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
)
write.csv(out, args[2], row.names = FALSE)
cat("Done\n")
Create patients.csv:
patient_id,age
001,54
002,61
003,47
Create Dockerfile:
FROM rocker/r-ver:4.3
WORKDIR /analysis
COPY summarize.R patients.csv ./
CMD ["Rscript", "summarize.R", "patients.csv", "summary.csv"]
Build and run:
docker build -t clinical-summary:1.0 .
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd):/output" clinical-summary:1.0 \
  sh -c "Rscript summarize.R patients.csv /output/summary.csv"
cat summary.csv
Expected output: CSV with n and mean_age.
Explanation: Image tag clinical-summary:1.0 documents the exact environment used.



Example 5 — Pull a bioinformatics tool
Problem: Run FastQC without manual installation.
Solution:
docker run --rm biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8 fastqc --help
Expected result: FastQC help text displayed.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
8. Real-world Industry Use Cases









#
Industry
Use case
How Docker helps




1
Bioinformatics
WGS variant calling
Pins BWA, GATK, bcftools versions


2
Pharma
Drug target ML models
Same model container in dev and prod


3
Clinical trials
TLF regeneration
Locked R/SAS environment per study


4
Healthcare
Diagnostic AI deployment
Isolated, scalable inference containers


5
CRO
Multi-study platforms
Separate containers per sponsor data


6
Manufacturing
Batch QC analytics
Validated image versions per SOP


7
Real-world evidence
Claims data pipelines
Reproducible ETL in the cloud


8
Genomics core lab
High-throughput FASTQ QC
Parallel containers per sample


9
Regulatory
Audit reproduction
Image digest proves what executed


10
Data engineering
ETL to data lake
Portable Spark/Python worker images


11
AI/ML
Model serving
FastAPI/Torch images on Kubernetes


12
Clinical programming
ADaM validation
Shared env for independent QC stats

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
9. Bioinformatics Workflow











Role of Docker: Each pipeline step (FastQC, alignment, calling) runs inside a container with an exact tool version. Nextflow orchestrates which container runs when. This prevents tool-version drift across samples and sites.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
10. Clinical Programming Workflow











How Docker helps:

SDTM/ADaM conversion — same R/Python/Pinnacle21 automation environment every lock
Analysis — reproducible rocker/tidyverse or SAS-compatible stacks
TLFs — QC statisticians re-run the identical container to verify outputs
Submission — image tags archived alongside analysis documentation







Warning



Docker supports reproducibility but does not replace formal CSV, SOPs, or 21 CFR Part 11 controls by itself.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
11. Best Practices

DO

Pin image versions (:1.0, :2024-06-28)
Store Dockerfiles in Git
Use multi-stage builds to reduce image size
Scan images for CVEs (Trivy, Snyk)
Mount data with volumes; keep PHI out of images
Document image digest in study files



DON’T

Use :latest in regulated production
Run containers as root when avoidable
Store passwords or API keys in Dockerfiles
Copy patient datasets into image layers
Expose unnecessary ports to the internet



Performance tips

Use -slim or -alpine base images
Leverage build cache; order Dockerfile layers from least to most frequently changed
Prune unused images: docker system prune



Security tips

Use private registries for internal images
Enable Docker Content Trust where required
Apply least-privilege IAM on cloud registries



Optimization tips

One process per container when possible
Use .dockerignore to speed builds
Compose multi-container apps with Docker Compose for local dev

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
12. Common Mistakes








Mistake
Reason
Solution




Docker Desktop not running
Service stopped after reboot
Launch Docker Desktop; wait for green status


permission denied on Linux
User not in docker group
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER


Huge images
Installing unnecessary packages
Use slim bases; multi-stage builds


Data lost after container stops
Wrote inside container filesystem
Use volumes for persistent output


Wrong architecture image
Apple Silicon vs x86 mismatch
Select arm64 or use --platform linux/amd64


PHI in image layers
Copied data in Dockerfile
Mount volumes at runtime only


Port already in use
Another service on same port
Change host port: -p 8081:8080


Cannot pull image
Network/proxy/firewall
Configure proxy; use internal mirror

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
13. Troubleshooting Guide








Error
Cause
Solution




Cannot connect to the Docker daemon
Docker not running
Start Docker Desktop / sudo systemctl start docker


pull access denied
Private image or auth needed
docker login to registry


no space left on device
Disk full
docker system df then docker system prune -a


exec format error
CPU architecture mismatch
Use correct platform image


port is already allocated
Port conflict
Stop other service or change mapping


Build fails at COPY
File missing from context
Check path and .dockerignore


Container exits immediately
CMD error
Run interactively: docker run -it image sh


Slow performance on Mac
File sharing overhead
Use delegated mounts; limit watched folders



Useful diagnostic commands:
docker ps -a
docker logs CONTAINER_ID
docker inspect IMAGE_NAME
docker system df
docker info

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
14. Interview Questions

Beginner
Q1: What is the difference between an image and a container?
A: An image is a read-only template; a container is a running instance of that image.
Q2: What is Docker Hub?
A: A public registry for storing and sharing container images.
Q3: Why do bioinformatics teams use Docker?
A: To pin tool versions and reproduce pipeline results across machines.


Intermediate
Q4: What is a volume and when would you use one?
A: Persistent storage mounted into a container — used for input data and output results without baking them into the image.
Q5: How do Docker and Kubernetes relate?
A: Docker builds/runs containers locally; Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across a cluster.
Q6: How would you make a clinical analysis reproducible with Docker?
A: Build a versioned image, document the tag/digest, mount study data at runtime, archive the Dockerfile in version control.


Advanced
Q7: What are multi-stage builds?
A: Dockerfiles with multiple FROM stages — compile in one stage, copy only artifacts to a smaller final image.
Q8: How do you reduce attack surface in production containers?
A: Non-root user, minimal base image, no secrets in layers, regular scanning, read-only filesystem where possible.
Q9: Explain how Nextflow uses Docker.
A: Each process specifies a container image; Nextflow pulls and runs it per task, enabling reproducible scalable pipelines.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
15. Cheat Sheet
# Info
docker --version
docker info

# Images
docker pull IMAGE:TAG
docker images
docker build -t NAME:TAG .
docker rmi IMAGE

# Containers
docker run --rm IMAGE
docker run -it --rm IMAGE sh
docker ps
docker ps -a
docker stop ID
docker logs ID
docker exec -it ID sh

# Data
docker run -v "$(pwd):/data" IMAGE

# Cleanup
docker system prune
docker system prune -a



Tip
Command




Remove container after exit
--rm


Interactive terminal
-it


Name a container
--name myjob


Map port
-p 8080:80


Env variable
-e VAR=value

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
16. Summary

Docker packages apps and dependencies into portable containers
An image is the template; a container is the running instance
Docker solves reproducibility, isolation, and deployment consistency
Life-sciences teams use Docker for genomics pipelines, clinical analysis, and ML
Install via Docker Desktop (Windows/Mac) or Docker Engine (Linux)
Build custom environments with a Dockerfile
Use volumes for data — never embed PHI in images
Pin version tags for regulated and scientific work
Combine Docker with Nextflow for scalable bioinformatics
Combine Docker with Quarto for reproducible reports
Scan images, prune disk usage, and document image digests
Troubleshoot with docker logs, docker ps, and docker inspect
Next recommended topics: Linux → Git → Nextflow → Kubernetes → AWS

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:

After Docker, learn Nextflow to orchestrate multi-step pipelines, or Linux foundations if you are new to the command line.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
section: 20. Mini Project — Reproducible Study Summary Container
text:
20. Mini Project — Reproducible Study Summary Container

Problem statement
Build a Dockerized R environment that reads a patient CSV and writes a summary CSV — suitable for QC documentation in a mock clinical study.


Architecture













Folder structure
clinical-docker-project/
├── Dockerfile
├── summarize.R
├── patients.csv
└── README.md


Commands
mkdir clinical-docker-project && cd clinical-docker-project
# Add Dockerfile, summarize.R, patients.csv from Example 4
docker build -t study-qc:1.0 .
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd):/output" study-qc:1.0 \
  sh -c "Rscript summarize.R patients.csv /output/summary.csv"


Expected output
summary.csv containing record count and mean age — reproducible when the same study-qc:1.0 image is used.






Tip



Archive the image tag and Git commit hash in your mock study documentation to practice GxP-style traceability.

title: Docker — Professional Tutorial for Life Sciences
text:
21. Exercises

10 practice questions

Define image vs container in your own words.
What command verifies Docker installation?
Why should you avoid :latest in production?
What flag removes a container after it exits?
How do you mount the current directory into a container?
Name two life-sciences registries or image sources.
Where does Docker fit in a FASTQ-to-VCF pipeline?
Why should PHI not be copied into a Dockerfile?
What command lists running containers?
How does Docker help clinical TLF QC?



5 assignments

Build an image with Python 3.11 that prints today’s date.
Run rocker/tidyverse and execute a dplyr pipeline inside the container.
Create a .dockerignore and explain what it excludes.
Pull a Biocontainers image and document its tag.
Write a one-page SOP for naming and tagging study images.



3 mini projects

Dockerize a simple FastQC command on a sample FASTQ file.
Build a two-stage Dockerfile (build tools → minimal runtime).
Compose a web app + database with Docker Compose locally.



1 capstone project
Design a reproducible mini pipeline: QC → summary stats → Quarto HTML report — each step in its own versioned container, orchestrated manually or with Nextflow.

Back to home

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
This page teaches you Quarto step-by-step. It is built for people who are new to Quarto and may not have any background in R.






Important



You do not need R to use Quarto. Start with plain Markdown and HTML output, then add code later if you want.




Quarto is an open-source publishing system that turns plain text into polished documents and websites.

Write content in Markdown
Add headings, lists, links, images, and tables
Render to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more




Quarto helps you:

create one document and output it in multiple formats
keep text and code together in one file
build websites, tutorials, and reports
publish clean content with version control




No.
Quarto works with:

plain Markdown only
Python
R
Julia
Observable JavaScript

For beginners, start with text-only documents.






Quarto
A text editor such as VS Code





Python or R if you want executable code blocks
A TeX engine like TinyTeX or TeX Live for PDF output




quarto --version
If Quarto is installed, this prints a version number.




Markdown is a simple text format that makes writing easy.


# Main title
## Section title
### Smaller section



**bold**
*italic*



- item one
- item two



[Quarto website](https://quarto.org)




Create a file named hello.qmd and add:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start



The section between --- lines is YAML. It sets the document title, format, and other options.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
format: html
---



Run this command:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto generates hello.html.



Quarto websites use multiple .qmd files and a _quarto.yml project file.
Example _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Quarto Website"
  navbar:
    right:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: tutorial.qmd
        text: Tutorial

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
Example index.qmd:
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.





![A sample image](images/example.png)



| Tool   | Purpose          |
|--------|------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents |
| VS Code| Edit files       |




You can add code blocks that only show code:
quarto render hello.qmd
If you install Python or R, Quarto can also run code and show results.




Code
print("Hello from Python")


Hello from Python






You can render HTML, PDF, and Word from one file.
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default




Forgetting the --- lines in YAML
Using the wrong file extension (.qmd instead of .txt)
Broken image paths
Trying to run code without installing Python or R





Install Quarto
Create a .qmd file
Add YAML and Markdown
Render to HTML
View the output
Edit and render again




Try these next steps:

Add another page like about.qmd
Use a custom theme or CSS
Add images, tables, and links
Experiment with format: docx or format: pdf


Great! Your Quarto website now contains a full beginner tutorial with examples and step-by-step guidance.

title: Linux — Foundations for Life Sciences Computing
text:
1. Introduction
Linux is the operating system that powers most servers, HPC clusters, and cloud instances used in bioinformatics and pharmaceutical computing.

Why Linux exists in your workflow
Clinical SAS/R environments may run on Windows, but cloud pipelines, sequencing cores, and DevOps tooling overwhelmingly run on Linux.


What you will learn here (upcoming)

Navigating the filesystem
Permissions, processes, and shell scripting
Connecting to remote servers (SSH)
Preparing for Docker, Git, and Nextflow

Back to home
